title: Patriarchy in Ha vs Feminism in Q
display_title: Patriarchy in Ha vs Feminism in Q
wikitext:
[]: Patriarchy in Ha vs Feminism in Q             Quote Post by Na7e » Sun Jul 28, 2013 6:59 pm [Split from "Why don't the Japanese like Rei Q?" - Monk] Bagheera wrote: And I'm still wondering how the heck he went from patriarchy all the way in Ha only to completely reverse course in Q. The whiplash makes my head hurt.

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Sun Jul 28, 2013 7:30 pm Yeah, 2.0 was pretty cringeworthy when it came to its treatment regarding females. And the male fans in particular ATE IT UP!

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Sun Jul 28, 2013 7:32 pm Well then, you can say that 3.0 perfectly made up for it : most of the female cast are with the good guys, and everyone important to the plot from WILLE are female(Misato, Ritsuko, Sakura, Asuka and Mari). And through the movie, everytime we had a big fight, it was the female pilots who fought : operation US(with a final participation from Shinji at the end), Asuka shoving the igniter and Misato then commandeering the Wunder to obliterate the NS, Rei Q raiding the Wunder to take Shinji and Mari trying to stop her, later Rei Q briefly clashing against Asuka before Mari pinned her, Asuka against pseudo-evolved Mark.09 saved the Wunder, and finally Mari jumping to catch EVA-13 mid-air and eject Shinji. The only time men were included in a fight where the technician aboard the Wunder(and they were subordinate to Misato, hell even the ship helmsman is a woman!) and the fight between Asuka and Shinji... where Shinji was completely on the defensive just blocking every blows from Asuka, even Kaworu "just" impaled the Eva with the spears and sacrificed himself. Clearly in 3.0 fighting and dirtying the hands are women business. Shinji may be the main character, but the heroes of the movies, the ones who fought and saved the day, were Asuka, Mari and Misato(and her crew... OK and Kaworu, but he didn't fought) I wonder how Final will turn out : keeping the trend(Asuka, Mari and Misato saving the world while Shinji philosophize about himself and maybe do something useful in the end, like in EoE) back to the somewhat patriarchal style of 2.0(Shinji saves the day), or maybe it will change again and we will have a confrontation human vs machines?(Asuka, Mari, Misato, maybe Rei and Shinji too against the army of MP-EVAs)

[]:             Quote Post by TheFriskyIan » Mon Jul 29, 2013 1:44 pm What?! This is actually a thing?! I'm having a hard time finding these events other than the whole "Cooking for Shinji" crap. Shinji did the exact same thing in NGE that he did in NME when it came to "growing a pair." This wasn't something new Anno threw in to "pander to the patriarchy!" Rei never cooked for Shinji cause of "Yippity Yappity she wants a date with him!" She did it for him and his father. I'm not so sure on Asuka, I still don't see where she fits in the whole "She acts the way girls are supposed too." If this was a movie trying to establish a patriarchy, it sure did a shitty job with Asuka taking the lead and acting above Shinji during her screen time like she did in NGE. As far as 3.0, it's even more non existent. Simply because the majority of the cast gets so little screen time that you can see their typical behavior, but as far as a mission goes, they don't really act any different from how they acted in 2.0 with the exception of Rei Q being a windup toy and Asuka being a bit more mature.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Jul 29, 2013 2:08 pm From the outside, it looks more like hetero in Ha vs homo in Q - complete with dykes v pooftahs for the final smackdown.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jul 29, 2013 2:10 pm Mr. Tines wrote: From the outside, it looks more like hetero in Ha vs homo in Q - complete with dykes v pooftahs for the final smackdown.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Mon Jul 29, 2013 2:44 pm The cooking scenes are still critical of patriarchy- notice that Rei and Asuka both injure their hands in the process, and consider the significance of hands on Evangelion (which is retained, and even strengthened, in Rebuild). By sinking to the level of cooking for him and "doing the right thing" and "stepping aside" for Rei, Asuka reduces herself to an object, going from a person to a point of contention between Shinji and his father. She has accepted and internalized her objecthood. By way of the Bardiel scene, Anno identifies making Asuka submissive with destroying her. The difference in their motivation is important- Asuka made herself submissive and cooked "for" Shinji to merely obtain him; Rei was cooking to achieve a greater goal than mere self-satisfaction. On some level Asuka recognizes this, which is why she steps aside. The intertextual references between 2.0 and the television series highlight this theme, most noticeably when Rei catches Asuka's slap in the elevator. Rei in 2.0 is actually very assertive, she's just not rude about it- she makes many decisions and sees them through, and her attack on Zeruel is motivated by principle, not by suicidal selflessness. The name change symbolizes Asuka and Rei becoming more similar in the Rebuild, and like the change of Yui and Gendo's last names, signifies changes in their role within the story- Asuka is now a secondary character as opposed to taking the focus of the narrative as she did in the original series.

[]:             Quote Post by TheFriskyIan » Mon Jul 29, 2013 3:07 pm Chuckman wrote: The cooking scenes are still critical of patriarchy- notice that Rei and Asuka both injure their hands in the process, and consider the significance of hands on Evangelion (which is retained, and even strengthened, in Rebuild). By sinking to the level of cooking for him and "doing the right thing" and "stepping aside" for Rei, Asuka reduces herself to an object, going from a person to a point of contention between Shinji and his father. She has accepted and internalized her objecthood. By way of the Bardiel scene, Anno identifies making Asuka submissive with destroying her. The difference in their motivation is important- Asuka made herself submissive and cooked "for" Shinji to merely obtain him; Rei was cooking to achieve a greater goal than mere self-satisfaction. On some level Asuka recognizes this, which is why she steps aside.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jul 29, 2013 3:19 pm Ian, you're completely missing the point. You're looking at the window dressing and mistaking it for the substance of what's being said and why. Eva, now as always, is full of symbols, and those symbols have meaning that goes far beyond the immediate story to comment on anime and Japanese culture as a whole. In this case that meaning is exactly as Chuckman said, along with ElMariachi and Gendo's Papa before him. It's blindingly obvious once you learn enough about the language of film to recognize it for what it is.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Jul 29, 2013 4:08 pm Eva is a story that challenges it's audience while critiquing anime culture. 2.0 represents a part of the story that is a parody of other typical anime, just as it was in the original series. If there's any reflection of patriarchy in 2.0, it is because most anime of this genre panders to a male audience. All the female characters in 2.0 are pretty much reflective of the "moe" trend, while Shinji finally steps into his hero shoes and makes a bold action for once. All of this is thrown back in the face of the audience in 3.0, and the gender roles are made more amiguous. Rei Q can be considered disturbing because she takes that submissive moe clone and takes all the moe out of it. And the rest of the female cast are strong and independent, while the male cast is mostly misguided and led astray.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Mon Jul 29, 2013 4:10 pm The reaction to Rei Q seems to be what Anno intended for the original Rei. Taking a beloved character (with a cute and appealing design) and making her disturbing is no mean feat.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Mon Jul 29, 2013 5:28 pm Well, what I meant is that I can see the feminism in Q(seriously, every female character except Sakura kick major ass!), but I don't really see the patriarchy in Ha, if only because even while Rei and Asuka were on a "cooking war", Shinji was still the housemaid : Who was seen cooking his, Asuka's, Misato's and Rei's meal? Shinji. Who was seen cleaning the dishes the day Asuka arrived? Shinji(and with an apron to boot!) Who was seen taking out the garbage and cleaning Misato's house in Jo? Shinji. Kaji even lampshades that Shinji's talent for cooking was a great thing to get the girls, and Toji's stubbornness that men shouldn't cook made him look like a moron. Meanwhile, Asuka and Rei has been seen doing one "girl" thing(cooking) through the whole movie. As for the fighting? The first two scenes of the movie were devoted to show Mari and later Asuka utterly curb-stomping an Angel on solo while getting their kick out of it! Shinji got his share against Sahaquiel which earned him Asuka's respect, and that wasn't a girl recognizing the boy's inherent strength, Shinji fucking deserved that respect, it take serious nerves to stop death an Eldritch Abomination falling like a comet right in your face, and continuing to do it while it was indirectly impaling your arms, and even then without the combined efforts of Rei and Asuka, he wouldn't have been able to win. Then comes Bardiel, a "Damsel in distress" situation if you want... and Shinji utterly failed by not being able to do anything, making Gendo activate the Dummy System that shredded EVA-03 to pieces and almost killed Asuka(and may have been the responsible for her "dead" eye in Q) Finally comes Zeruel who crushes every resistance, even from the woman pilots(Mari and Rei), until super Shinji comes to the rescue... yes but hadn't the two female pilots gained some time by fighting him, Shinji would have never been able to get into EVA-01, and Mari was the one who let him have his epiphany that running away is not the solution. And in the end, as awesomely epic his fight has been, it still led to catastrophic consequences, starting Third Impact right on the spot, and mankind owe his survival only to Kaworu's intervention(who's not even really human...) Such a great male lead he has been...

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Mon Jul 29, 2013 6:18 pm Chuckman wrote: Rei in 2.0 is actually very assertive, she's just not rude about it- she makes many decisions and sees them through, and her attack on Zeruel is motivated by principle, not by suicidal selflessness.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Jul 29, 2013 6:33 pm Na7e wrote: Man did you miss the part where we get a cut of Shinji's S-Dat, and her saying she's doing this so Shinji will never have to pilot again. In conjunction with the elevator scene which is also a major moment of Rei's character it's pretty clear that they were building her up to be self-sacrificial for her man.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jul 29, 2013 6:53 pm Sachi, you're looking at plot when you should be looking at role. Ha is patriarchal because of the roles the girls play, not because of what happens to them in the story. Rei II has no purpose whatsoever beyond advancing Shinji's story. Nothing. Nada. Zip. Asuka's in the same boat: when she arrived she appeared to have a backstory and her own goals, but she set these aside so she could get Bardieled. Why? Again, for the sole reason of advancing Shinji's story. Everything that happened to the girls happened for Shinji's sake, not their own. Now, look at Q. Does ReiQ start her introspective journey for Shinji, or for herself? Is Asuka fighting to destroy Shinji, or is she fighting to destroy Nerv and only targeting Shinji because he happens to be in her way? And what about Misato? In Ha she was cheering him on, doing nothing of note herself, while in Q she's commanding Wille in a mission utterly devoid of Shinji. Even her failure to active the bomb collar says little to nothing about him and volumes about her. Her ship. Her mission. Her story. Shinji's a part of it, but he's not the center of her world the way he was in Ha. That's the difference. We're talking about structural patterns here, not specific events. That's how patriarchy works.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Jul 29, 2013 7:23 pm So, the difference is that in 2.0 the female characters roles revolve around furthering Shinji's story, while in 3.0 their roles are independent of his? That seems pretty unavoidable when the plot of 2.0 governs that Shinji builds relationships with others, while 3.0's plot governs that Shinji becomes continuously isolated from others. Again, this is a result of the plot. Yes, the roles in 2.0 can be described as patriarchal (Shinji being the patriarch), but that's just an adjective to describe something inherent in Shinji's story, not something that was necessarily put there.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jul 29, 2013 7:49 pm Sachi wrote: So, the difference is that in 2.0 the female characters roles revolve around furthering Shinji's story, while in 3.0 their roles are independent of his? That seems pretty unavoidable when the plot of 2.0 governs that Shinji builds relationships with others, while 3.0's plot governs that Shinji becomes continuously isolated from others. Again, this is a result of the plot. Yes, the roles in 2.0 can be described as patriarchal (Shinji being the patriarch), but that's just an adjective to describe something inherent in Shinji's story, not something that was necessarily put there.

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Mon Jul 29, 2013 8:09 pm Anno is clearly a director interested in gender roles in the anime industry. Particularly female gender roles. You look at his works like Gunbuster, NGE, His & Her Circumstances, Love & Pop, Shiki-Jitsu & to a lesser extent Nadia & Cutie Honey & it's there. Rebuild as a whole is also playing around with those gender roles in vast ways & I find it to be a fascinating piece of the overall work. Anno knows what he's doing with Rebuild as a whole & each of the four films has it's part to play in the piece. For a writer who places such importance on the strength of his female characters I'd argue Anno intentionally had 2.0 fail the BECHDEL TEST. It panders to the male viewer - the male ANIME viewer especially - in grand ways. [url]http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bechdel_Test[/url] 3.0 meanwhile has but one quick moment when Mari makes a joke towards Asuka about her emotions towards Shinji & she quickly brushes it off as unimportant. Which in the grand scheme of things IT IS. These people are fighting for the existance of the human race. Finding a partner is low on the totem pole. Gender roles are a huge part of NGE & Rebuild particularly because it's in sharp contrast to what the rest of anime as a medium seems to represent. You want independent minded female characters in an anime who aren't defined by their male counterpart & act based on their own impulses they basically have to come from early Ghibli works or exist in a fictional universe where male characters are practically nonexistant. As an act of pandering to the anime fan the Test-Suit (i.e. Slut Suit) Asuka wears is pretty genius. Especially since it's in no way out of place in a medium that generally has "strong-willed" women dress like strippers & has legions of fans who can talk about "great panty & upskirt moments". I'm passionate about this whole issue. As a male filmmaker it's always an issue I have to confront when dealing with constructing female characters - is the female character I've written an actual human being with a life of her own or have I just written a wish-fulfillment dream girl who exists only for her male counterpart? - & watching what Anno has done with the three Rebuilds to date has only made me excited about him as a filmmaker all the more. He's among my the top tier echelon of filmmakers working today for a reason! I'm excited to see how he'll carry this on in the last act of Rebuild & while I do anticipate & want the female characters to be more loving towards Shinji in 4.0 - Asuka & Misato especially since it's integral to their emotional journies in the narrative - I don't want to see them "baking Shinji a cake so he feels better" or cliche anime tropes like that. Tropes that 2.0 intentionally utilized to soothe fans into a false sense of security. Hell, one of the many things I love about Mari as a character is she doesn't exist to be the third female pilot in Shinji's "harem". She doesn't necessarily pay him no mind but she doesn't hate him either. She just exists in the same universe as he does. I should sit down & really write a book on this sometime.... if I only had time. :)

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Mon Jul 29, 2013 8:50 pm this thread offends me and should be banned

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Mon Jul 29, 2013 9:22 pm Bagheera wrote: Sachi, you're looking at plot when you should be looking at role. Ha is patriarchal because of the roles the girls play, not because of what happens to the in the story. Rei II has no purpose whatsoever beyond advancing Shinji's story. Nothing. Nada. Zip. Asuka's in the same boat: when she arrived she appeared to have a backstory and her own goals, but she set these aside so she could get Bardieled. Why? Again, for the sole reason of advancing Shinji's story. Everything that happened to the girls happened for Shinji's sake, not their own. Now, look at Q. Does ReiQ start her introspective journey for Shinji, or for herself? Is Asuka fighting to destroy Shinji, or is she fighting to destroy Nerv and only targeting Shinji because he happens to be in her way? And what about Misato? In Ha she was cheering him on, doing nothing of note herself, while in Q she's commanding Wille in a mission utterly devoid of Shinji. Even her failure to active the bomb collar says little to nothing about him and volumes about her. Her ship. Her mission. Her story. Shinji's a part of it, but he's not the center of her world the way he was in Ha. That's the difference. We're talking about structural patterns here, not specific events. That's how patriarchy works.

Rei never cooked for Shinji cause of "Yippity Yappity she wants a date with him!" She did it for him and his father. By sinking to the level of cooking for him and "doing the right thing" and "stepping aside" for Rei, Asuka reduces herself to an object, going from a person to a point of contention between Shinji and his father. She has accepted and internalized her objecthood. By way of the Bardiel scene, Anno identifies making Asuka submissive with destroying her. The difference in their motivation is important- Asuka made herself submissive and cooked "for" Shinji to merely obtain him; Rei was cooking to achieve a greater goal than mere self-satisfaction. On some level Asuka recognizes this, which is why she steps aside. The intertextual references between 2.0 and the television series highlight this theme, most noticeably when Rei catches Asuka's slap in the elevator. Rei in 2.0 is actually very assertive, she's just not rude about it- she makes many decisions and sees them through, and her attack on Zeruel is motivated by principle, not by suicidal selflessness. The name change symbolizes Asuka and Rei becoming more similar in the Rebuild, and like the change of Yui and Gendo's last names, signifies changes in their role within the story- Asuka is now a secondary character as opposed to taking the focus of the narrative as she did in the original series.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jul 29, 2013 6:53 pm Sachi, you're looking at plot when you should be looking at role. Ha is patriarchal because of the roles the girls play, not because of what happens to them in the story. Rei II has no purpose whatsoever beyond advancing Shinji's story. Nothing. Nada. Zip. Asuka's in the same boat: when she arrived she appeared to have a backstory and her own goals, but she set these aside so she could get Bardieled.

Article: Patriarchy in Ha vs Feminism in Q
 whatsoever beyond advancing Shinji's story. Nothing. Nada. Zip. Asuka's in the same boat: when she arrived she appeared to have a backstory and her own goals, but she set these aside so she could get Bardieled. Why? Again, for the sole reason of advancing Shinji's story. Everything that happened to the girls happened for Shinji's sake, not their own. Now, look at Q. Does ReiQ start her introspective journey for Shinji, or for herself? Is Asuka fighting to destroy Shinji, or is she fighting to destroy Nerv and only targeting Shinji because he happens to be in her way? And what about Misato? In Ha she was cheering him on, doing nothing of note herself, while in Q she's commanding Wille in a mission utterly devoid of Shinji. Even her failure to active the bomb collar says little to nothing about him and volumes about her. Her ship. Her mission. Her story. Shinji's a part of it, but he's not the center of her world the way he was in Ha. That's the difference. We're talking about structural patterns here, not specific events. That's how patriarchy works.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Jul 29, 2013 7:23 pm So, the difference is that in 2.0 the female characters roles revolve around furthering Shinji's story, while in 3.0 their roles are independent of his? That seems pretty unavoidable when the plot of 2.0 governs that Shinji builds relationships with others, while 3.0's plot governs that Shinji becomes continuously isolated from others. Again, this is a result of the plot. Yes, the roles in 2.0 can be described as patriarchal (Shinji being the patriarch), but that's just an adjective to describe something inherent in Shinji's story, not something that was necessarily put there.

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Mon Jul 29, 2013 8:09 pm Anno is clearly a director interested in gender roles in the anime industry. Particularly female gender roles. You look at his works like Gunbuster, NGE, His & Her Circumstances, Love & Pop, Shiki-Jitsu & to a lesser extent Nadia & Cutie Honey & it's there. Rebuild as a whole is also playing around with those gender roles in vast ways & I find it to be a fascinating piece of the overall work. Anno knows what he's doing with Rebuild as a whole & each of the four films has it's part to play in the piece. For a writer who places such importance on the strength of his female characters I'd argue Anno intentionally had 2.0 fail the BECHDEL TEST. It panders to the male viewer - the male ANIME viewer especially - in grand ways. [url]http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bechdel_Test[/url] 3.0 meanwhile has but one quick moment when Mari makes a joke towards Asuka about her emotions towards Shinji & she quickly brushes it off as unimportant. Which in the grand scheme of things IT IS. These people are fighting for the existance of the human race. Finding a partner is low on the totem pole. Gender roles are a huge part of NGE & Rebuild particularly because it's in sharp contrast to what the rest of anime as a medium seems to represent. You want independent minded female characters in an anime who aren't defined by their male counterpart & act based on their own impulses they basically have to come from early Ghibli works or exist in a fictional universe where male characters are practically nonexistant. As an act of pandering to the anime fan the Test-Suit (i.e. Slut Suit) Asuka wears is pretty genius. Especially since it's in

Article: Patriarchy in Ha vs Feminism in Q
li works or exist in a fictional universe where male characters are practically nonexistant. As an act of pandering to the anime fan the Test-Suit (i.e. Slut Suit) Asuka wears is pretty genius. Especially since it's in no way out of place in a medium that generally has "strong-willed" women dress like strippers & has legions of fans who can talk about "great panty & upskirt moments". I'm passionate about this whole issue. As a male filmmaker it's always an issue I have to confront when dealing with constructing female characters - is the female character I've written an actual human being with a life of her own or have I just written a wish-fulfillment dream girl who exists only for her male counterpart? - & watching what Anno has done with the three Rebuilds to date has only made me excited about him as a filmmaker all the more. He's among my the top tier echelon of filmmakers working today for a reason! I'm excited to see how he'll carry this on in the last act of Rebuild & while I do anticipate & want the female characters to be more loving towards Shinji in 4.0 - Asuka & Misato especially since it's integral to their emotional journies in the narrative - I don't want to see them "baking Shinji a cake so he feels better" or cliche anime tropes like that. Tropes that 2.0 intentionally utilized to soothe fans into a false sense of security. Hell, one of the many things I love about Mari as a character is she doesn't exist to be the third female pilot in Shinji's "harem". She doesn't necessarily pay him no mind but she doesn't hate him either. She just exists in the same universe as he does. I should sit down & really write a book on this sometime.... if I only had time. :)
